comments.text: Let $f(n)$ be the largest integer such that every integer $N \ge n$ can be written as a sum of distinct $3$-smooth integers, all larger than $f(n)$, and such that no two distinct elements divide each other. Then the remarks mention somewhat recent bounds on $f(n)$ by Yu-Chen and Yang-Zhao. However, essentially optimal bounds were already obtained back in $1998$ by Blecksmith, McCallum and Selfridge. R. Blecksmith, M. McCallum, J.L. Selfridge, 3-smooth representations of integers. The American Mathematical Monthly, Volume 105, No. 6, 529–543, 1998. You can find their paper here. In particular, their Theorem $2$ states that every integer larger than or equal to $$2 \sum_{2^a3^b \le m} 2^a 3^b$$can be written as a sum of distinct $3$-smooth integers, all larger than $m$, and such that no two distinct elements divide each other. On the other hand, every such way of writing the integer $$2 \sum_{2^a3^b \le m} 2^a 3^b - 1$$contains an element smaller than or equal to $m$. If I'm not mistaken, this gives $$f(n) = \left(\frac{\log 2 \log 3}{2} + o(1) \right) \frac{n}{\log n}.$$ (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

The statement should be 'Yu and Chen [YuCh22] prove that the set of representable numbers has density zero...' instead of 'non-representable numbers'. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

There is a paper 'A conjecture of Yu and Chen related to the Erdős–Lewin theorem' by Quan-Hui Yang and Lilu Zhao in Acta Arithmetica 217 (2025) which proves that there is a constant $c>0$ such that one can take $f(n)\gg\frac{cn}{\log n}$. A result in Yu and Chen's paper shows that this optimal up to the constant. The Yu and Chen paper also proves that there are infinitely many coprime non-representable numbers when $\{p,q\}\neq \{2,3\},\{2,5\},\{2,9\},\{3,5\}$. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

who proved that there are infinitely many non-representable numbers if and only if ... probably should be who proved that there are finitely many non-representable numbers if and only if (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
